title: Lengths of Closed Geodesics in Manifolds of Positive Scalar Curvature
abstract: I will prove Gromov's conjecture that every 3-manifold of positive scalar curvature contains a short closed geodesic. The proof uses Min-Max theory of minimal surfaces and a combinatorial version of mean curvature flow. Time permitting, I will describe other results about geometry and topology of 3-manifolds proved using minimal surfaces. This is a joint work with Davi Maximo and Regina Rotman.

proof_stages.label: Positive-scalar-curvature closed-geodesic problem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the Gromov-type question and the three-dimensional theorem bounding the length of some closed geodesic under a scalar-curvature lower bound, then explains that scalar curvature is detected not by geodesics directly but through stable minimal hypersurfaces, suggesting a strategy of cutting the manifold by controlled surfaces and then by controlled curves.

proof_stages.label: Loop-space minimax from short sweepouts
proof_stages.summary: Closed geodesics are obtained either by minimizing in a nontrivial free homotopy class or, in the simply connected case, from a noncontractible family of loops of length at most L via curve-shortening/Morse-theoretic minimax.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative sweepouts of 2-spheres
proof_stages.summary: The talk reviews the two-dimensional quantitative input: area alone and diameter alone do not bound closed-curve sweepouts, area plus diameter does, and if one allows flat one-cycles then area control alone suffices.

proof_stages.label: Topological reduction and reducible 3-manifolds
proof_stages.summary: After reducing to orientable manifolds and invoking the classification of positive-scalar-curvature 3-manifolds, the reducible case is handled by minimizing a nontrivial embedded 2-sphere to a stable minimal sphere, using stability to bound its area and diameter, sweeping it out by short curves, and applying loop minimax.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to S3 and minimal-surface decomposition
proof_stages.summary: The irreducible case reduces from spherical space forms to S3, where a bumpy-metric perturbation, stable minimal spheres, and a surface minmax argument produce pieces bounded by stable spheres and a unique index-one minimal sphere, all with controlled area and diameter.

proof_stages.label: L-shortness as substitute for intrinsic diameter control
proof_stages.summary: Mean-curvature flow gives area-controlled foliations but may lose diameter control, while one-cycle sweepouts can lead only to geodesic nets, so the proof introduces an intermediate Rotman-style notion of L-short surfaces, where curves on the surface can be coned to a basepoint by short ambient arcs.

proof_stages.label: L-short foliation and final geodesic bound
proof_stages.summary: The final propositions assert that an L-short foliation by 2-spheres yields a short closed geodesic and that local area-reducing replacements away from minimal surfaces allow an iterative construction from an index-one sphere to the stable boundary spheres while preserving the needed shortness, thereby sweeping out S3 and completing the proof.

examples.name: Nearly round ellipsoid sharpness example
examples.note: The ellipsoid illustrates the classical two-dimensional theorem's sharpness by showing that after the three simple closed geodesics, further closed geodesics can be very long and self-intersecting.
examples.engagement_reason: The ellipsoid is invoked only to state sharpness and that a fourth geodesic can be long and self-intersecting, without working through the example.

passes.reason: Near-round ellipsoids are used as a sharpness family showing a fourth short simple geodesic cannot be guaranteed.
the near-round ellipsoid realizes the sharp minimum of three simple closed geodesics and rules out a four-simple-geodesic strengthening
A nearly round ellipsoid is a tight limiting sharpness case, used to show one cannot demand a fourth short embedded geodesic.
near-round ellipsoid is a tight sharpness metric showing a fourth short simple geodesic need not exist
A near-round triaxial ellipsoid is a sharp limiting family showing a stronger fourth short/simple geodesic statement cannot be guaranteed.
A near-round triaxial ellipsoid is a sharp/tight case with only three short/simple geodesics, used to rule out a fourth-geodesic strengthening.
A near-round triaxial ellipsoid realizes the sharp minimum of three simple short geodesics and blocks strengthening to a fourth.
A limiting ellipsoid family is used to show the three-geodesic theorem cannot be strengthened to a short fourth simple closed geodesic.
used to show the three-simple-geodesic theorem cannot be strengthened to a fourth short simple geodesic, with the ellipsoidal family realizing the sharp/minimal case
near-round triaxial ellipsoid is a sharp/extremal case ruling out a strengthening to a fourth short simple closed geodesic
Used to show a fourth short/simple geodesic cannot be forced, with a near-round ellipsoid forming a tight sharpness family.
Used to show the three-geodesic statement cannot be strengthened to a fourth simple short geodesic, and it is a sharp/minimal geodesic-count case.

examples.name: Latitude sweepout of the standard 2-sphere
examples.note: The family of latitude circles from the south pole to the north pole makes the abstract loop-space sweepout definition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly constructs the sweepout by mapping each parameter to the intersection of the standard 2-sphere with a horizontal plane, yielding latitude circles from pole to pole.

passes.reason: The latitude family is the canonical highly regular sweepout of the round two-sphere.

examples.name: Large-radius round sphere
examples.note: In response to a question, scaling a round sphere illustrates why the closed-geodesic length bound must deteriorate as the scalar-curvature lower bound tends to zero.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the large-radius round sphere concretely to reason that as the radius grows the scalar-curvature lower bound tends to zero while the closed geodesic length tends to infinity.

passes.reason: Round spheres are maximally symmetric and, as the radius grows, form a limiting family with unbounded geodesic length absent a curvature lower bound.
large round spheres are used to show that no uniform short-geodesic bound survives as the curvature scale goes to zero
Round spheres are maximally symmetric, and taking the radius large approaches the zero-curvature long-geodesic boundary to refute a curvature-free length bound.
large-radius round spheres form a symmetric limiting family with shortest geodesics unbounded, ruling out a curvature-free bound
A large round sphere is maximally symmetric and is used to show no uniform geodesic-length bound exists without a scalar-curvature lower bound.
A large round sphere is maximally symmetric and is used to show no uniform short-geodesic bound exists without a curvature scale.
Scaling the round sphere to large radius gives a maximally symmetric limiting family with closed-geodesic length unbounded as curvature tends to zero.
Large scaled round spheres are maximally symmetric limiting cases as scalar curvature tends to zero, refuting curvature-free length bounds.
large round spheres form a maximally symmetric large-scale limiting family showing no uniform closed-geodesic length bound without a curvature lower bound
large round spheres are symmetric scaling-limit examples showing no curvature-free uniform upper bound on geodesic length
Used to rule out a curvature-free length bound, and the round spheres are maximally symmetric with large radius approaching the zero-curvature boundary.
Used to rule out a uniform geodesic-length bound as the scalar-curvature lower bound tends to zero; the round large-radius family is maximally symmetric and limiting toward the flat boundary.

examples.name: Long thin starfish 2-sphere
examples.note: The long-tentacled small-area sphere shows that area alone cannot force short closed-curve sweepouts, motivating the need for diameter control or for allowing disconnected one-cycles.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through the long thin starfish sphere, explaining why any sweepout must climb a long tentacle and how thinness keeps area small while forcing large lengths.

passes.reason: The collapsed long thin tentacles give an extreme small-area but large-sweepout-width sphere refuting an area-only bound.
the long thin starfish is a collapsed high-aspect-ratio metric showing that small area alone cannot bound sweepout length
The long thin starfish sphere is a collapsed high-aspect-ratio limiting metric, used as a worst case against area-only sweepout control.
collapsed long thin tentacles give small area but arbitrarily long required sweepouts, disproving an area-only bound
The long thin starfish sphere is a collapsing extremal-aspect-ratio metric disproving area-only control of sweepout length.
The long thin starfish is a collapsing/tentacular limiting metric giving the sharp large-diameter obstruction to area-only sweepout bounds.
The long thin tentacled sphere collapses toward a graph and defeats any area-only bound for closed-curve sweepouts.
The collapsed long-tentacle sphere makes fixed small area compatible with arbitrarily large sweepout length.
the long thin tentacles collapse toward a graph-like limit and give an extreme small-area but large-sweepout-width obstruction to area-only bounds
long thin tentacles form a collapsed extremal family showing area alone cannot bound sweepout length
Used to refute area-only sweepout bounds, with a long thin sphere collapsing toward a graph-like limiting shape and making the diameter obstruction extreme.
Used to disprove an area-only sweepout bound, with a collapsed high-aspect-ratio thin-tentacle metric forcing the sharp diameter dependence.

examples.name: Tree-wall metric on a 2-sphere
examples.note: The thickened-tree construction shows that bounded diameter alone cannot bound sweepout length and explains the logarithmic dependence on area in the area-diameter sweepout theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes the tree-wall metric construction and analyzes how contractions must cross many tree edges, relating sweepout length to tree height and area to number of edges.

passes.reason: The graph-like tree-wall metric is a collapsed sharp obstruction with small diameter but sweepout length forced by tree height.
the tree-wall sphere is a graph-like collapsed construction giving the sharp logarithmic obstruction to diameter-only sweepout bounds

examples.name: Stationary geodesic net obstruction
examples.note: The possible one-cycle minimax limit with vertices or twisted loops shows why one-cycle sweepouts do not automatically yield a closed geodesic and prompts the stronger loop-level strategy.
examples.engagement_reason: Stationary geodesic nets are cited as the possible min-max output for one-cycles, with only schematic descriptions of vertices or twisted loops and no concrete analysis.

passes.reason: A stationary geodesic net can arise from one-cycle min-max instead of a closed geodesic, obstructing that approach.
stationary geodesic nets arise as the unwanted min-max outputs that obstruct replacing loop sweepouts by one-cycle sweepouts
A stationary geodesic net is a possible singular min-max output obstructing the desired conclusion that a closed geodesic is obtained.
a stationary geodesic net is a variational critical one-cycle that can arise instead of a smooth closed geodesic, obstructing the proposed min-max route
A stationary geodesic net is a singular limiting one-cycle that obstructs obtaining a smooth closed geodesic from one-cycle min-max.
A stationary geodesic net is a typical singular geodesic network and is invoked as the obstruction showing 1-cycle min-max need not yield a closed geodesic.
A stationary geodesic net is an ordinary possible one-cycle min-max output obstructing the desired closed-geodesic conclusion.
A stationary geodesic net is the possible non-geodesic min-max outcome that obstructs using one-cycle sweepouts to get a closed geodesic.
it is used as an obstruction since one-cycle min-max can yield a stationary geodesic network rather than a smooth closed geodesic
a singular geodesic net with junctions or twists obstructs the one-cycle min-max route to an actual closed geodesic
An obstruction to the one-cycle min-max construction producing a closed geodesic, and its stationarity makes it a variational critical object.
